articles.title: Graph Neural Networks for Natural Language Processing: A Survey
articles.authors: Wu L; Chen Y; Shen K; Zhang X; Xue H; Zhang S; Tan J; Liu B
articles.keywords: Graph Neural Networks; NLP; deep learning; graph construction; encoder-decoder; survey
articles.abstract: Deep learning has become the dominant approach in NLP. Although text inputs are typically represented as sequences, many NLP problems can be best expressed with graph structures. This survey presents a comprehensive overview of GNNs for NLP, proposing a new taxonomy along three axes: graph construction, graph representation learning, and graph-based encoder-decoder models. A large number of NLP applications exploiting GNNs are introduced, along with benchmark datasets, evaluation metrics, and open-source codes. Outstanding challenges and future research directions are discussed. To the best of the authors' knowledge, this is the first comprehensive overview of GNNs for NLP.

articles.title: Graph Neural Networks for Text Classification: A Survey
articles.authors: Wang K; Ding Y; Han SC
articles.keywords: GNN; text classification; NLP; graph construction; representation learning
articles.abstract: Text classification is the most fundamental problem in NLP. While many recent models apply sequential deep learning, GNN-based models can directly handle complex structured text data and exploit global information. This survey covers methods up to 2023, including corpus-level and document-level GNNs. Each method is discussed in detail regarding graph construction mechanisms and the graph-based learning process. Issues and future directions for text classification using GNNs are addressed, together with datasets, evaluation metrics, experiment design, and a summary of published performance on public benchmarks.

articles.title: Graph Prompt Learning: A Comprehensive Survey and Beyond
articles.authors: Sun X; Zhang J; Wu X; Cheng H; Xiong Y; Li J
articles.keywords: graph prompt; graph pre-training; GNN; AGI; NLP; cross-modality
articles.abstract: AGI has revolutionized numerous fields, yet its integration with graph data remains nascent. This pioneering survey on graph prompts in AGI addresses key challenges and opportunities in harnessing graph data for AGI applications. Despite advancements in AGI across NLP and CV, the application to graphs is underexplored. The survey critically evaluates the current landscape of AGI in handling graph data, highlighting challenges in cross-modality, cross-domain, and cross-task applications. A unified framework is proposed for understanding graph prompt learning, covering prompt tokens, token structures, and insertion patterns. A comprehensive taxonomy categorizes over 100 works, aligning them with pre-training tasks across node-level, edge-level, and graph-level objectives. The Python library ProG is also presented.

articles.title: A Survey of Graph Neural Networks in Real world: Imbalance, Noise, Privacy and OOD Challenges
articles.authors: Ju W; et al.
articles.keywords: GNN; graph neural networks; real-world; imbalance; noise; privacy; OOD
articles.abstract: Graph-structured data exhibits universality across domains such as social networks, biochemistry, financial fraud detection, and network security. However, training GNNs in real-world scenarios suffers from unfavorable factors including class imbalance, noisy data, privacy constraints, and out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization. This survey comprehensively reviews efforts to improve GNN performance under these challenges, enhancing reliability and robustness. Two versions were released: v1 (March 2024) with 11,722 KB and v2 (November 2025) with major revisions.

articles.title: Reasoning on Graphs: Faithful and Interpretable Large Language Model Reasoning
articles.authors: Luo L; Li Y; Haffari G; Pan S
articles.keywords: LLM; knowledge graph; reasoning; KGQA; faithful; interpretable; NLP
articles.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are often unreliable due to lack of knowledge and hallucination. Knowledge Graphs (KGs) capture abundant factual knowledge in structured format, offering a faithful knowledge source for reasoning. This paper introduces RoG (Reasoning on Graphs), a framework that synergizes LLMs with KGs to enable faithful and interpretable reasoning. RoG first generates relation paths grounded on KGs as faithful plans, then retrieves valid reasoning paths from the KG to conduct faithful reasoning. Extensive experiments on knowledge graph question answering datasets (WebQSP and CWQ) demonstrate that RoG outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both reasoning and generalization to unseen data.

articles.title: Graph-constrained Reasoning: Faithful Reasoning on Knowledge Graphs with Large Language Models
articles.authors: Luo L; Ju W; et al. (Monash University, Nanjing University of Science and Technology)
articles.keywords: LLM; knowledge graph; hallucination; GCR; constrained decoding; faithful reasoning
articles.abstract: LLMs struggle with faithful reasoning due to knowledge gaps and hallucinations, causing factual errors. This paper introduces Graph-Constrained Reasoning (GCR), a KG-guided reasoning paradigm that connects unstructured LLM reasoning with structured KG knowledge, eliminating hallucinations. GCR encodes KG structure into LLMs via a KG-specialized tokenizer, enabling end-to-end reasoning tightly coupled to graph structure. A self-consistency mechanism generates multiple reasoning paths and selects the most consistent answer. GCR achieves state-of-the-art performance on KGQA benchmarks while significantly reducing hallucination errors.

articles.title: LLMs for Knowledge Graph Construction and Reasoning: Recent Capabilities and Future Opportunities
articles.authors: Zhu Y; Wang X; Chen J; Qiao S; Ou Y; Yao Y; Deng S; Chen H; Zhang N
articles.keywords: LLM; knowledge graph; entity extraction; relation extraction; link prediction; question answering
articles.abstract: This paper empirically investigates the potential of LLMs (ChatGPT, GPT-4) for KG construction and reasoning tasks. It evaluates entity and relation extraction, event extraction, link prediction, and knowledge-based QA. Analysis reveals where LLMs excel and fall short compared to fine-tuned specialized models. Future opportunities are identified including few-shot KG construction, hallucination-aware reasoning, and integrating LLMs with structured knowledge representations. Results show LLMs possess emerging KG reasoning capabilities but still underperform specialized models on complex structured tasks.

articles.title: Reasoning with Graphs: Structuring Implicit Knowledge to Enhance LLMs Reasoning
articles.authors: Jiang D; Xu X; Ye W; et al.
articles.keywords: graph-of-thought; LLM reasoning; implicit knowledge; structured reasoning; CoT; GoT
articles.abstract: LLMs still struggle with multi-step reasoning tasks requiring integration of implicit world knowledge. This paper proposes structuring implicit knowledge as graphs during inference to enhance LLM reasoning. The approach converts unstructured implicit knowledge into explicit graph representations, enabling Graph-of-Thought (GoT) reasoning. Compared to Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Tree-of-Thought (ToT), the graph-based structuring allows more flexible traversal of reasoning paths. Extensive experiments on commonsense reasoning, multi-hop QA, and mathematical reasoning tasks demonstrate consistent improvements over strong baselines.

articles.title: From Text to Graph: Leveraging Graph Neural Networks for Enhanced Explainability in NLP
articles.authors: García-Silva A; et al.
articles.keywords: GNN; NLP; explainability; text-to-graph; constituency relations; knowledge base; LLM
articles.abstract: LLMs have proven useful for many NLP tasks, but most remain opaque black boxes. This paper proposes converting text to graph structures via constituency and dependency parsing to explicitly represent implicit knowledge in LLM sentences. The resulting graphs are processed with GNNs to produce traceable, explainable representations. The method enables knowledge base population with increased traceability, making it possible to perform model analytics and diagnose model behavior. Evaluated on standard NLP benchmarks, the approach improves explainability metrics while maintaining competitive performance on classification and relation extraction.

articles.title: High-Modularity Graph Partitioning Through NLP Techniques and Maximal Clique Enumeration
articles.authors: Not fully disclosed (MUR PRIN Projects EXPAND and NextGRAAL)
articles.keywords: community detection; graph partitioning; NLP; clique enumeration; modularity; network analytics
articles.abstract: Community detection is a fundamental tool in network analytics with applications in social networks, biology, cybersecurity, and financial networks. This paper proposes a novel high-modularity graph partitioning method combining NLP techniques with maximal clique enumeration. Graph-theoretic properties based on cliques (k-plexes, s-defective, γ-quasi cliques) model strict community definitions. NLP-derived semantic similarity is used to guide clique-based partitioning, achieving higher modularity scores than traditional methods. Evaluated on benchmark network datasets, the method outperforms Louvain and Leiden algorithms on modularity while being interpretable via NLP feature attribution.

articles.title: Research Trends for the Interplay between Large Language Models and Knowledge Graphs
articles.authors: Khorashadizadeh H; Amara FZ; Ezzabady M; et al.
articles.keywords: LLM; knowledge graph; NLP; interplay; survey; VLDB
articles.abstract: LLMs and Knowledge Graphs (KGs) are two pivotal technologies driving advances in machine understanding, reasoning, and NLP. LLMs such as GPT generate human-like text, answer questions, and create content. KGs organize information structurally, enabling inference over entity relationships. This paper surveys research trends at the intersection of LLMs and KGs, covering KG-enhanced LLM reasoning, LLM-driven KG construction, and hybrid architectures. Published at the VLDB 2024 LLM+KG Workshop, it identifies open challenges including incomplete KGs, hallucination, and scalability.

articles.title: Biomedical Hypothesis Explainability with Graph-Based Context Retrieval (HGCR)
articles.authors: Not fully disclosed
articles.keywords: biomedical; hypothesis generation; graph theory; NLP; LLM; explainability; MEDLINE
articles.abstract: Biomedical hypothesis generation (HG) systems increasingly lack interpretability. HGCR (Hypothesis Generation Context Retriever) is proposed as a graph-based context retrieval system that constructs semantic paths from the entire MEDLINE dataset of abstracts to explain potential relationships between biomedical concepts. A contrastive training objective distinguishes meaningful reasoning chains from corrupted paths. A feedback loop uses AGATHA and SemRep to validate semantic predicates (subject-verb-object triples) from LLM outputs. Unsupported claims trigger context update and iterative refinement. The method improves explanation quality and factual grounding in biomedical hypothesis generation.

articles.title: Natural Language Processing and Graph Theory: Making Sense of Imaging Records in a Novel Representation Frame
articles.authors: Gonçalves LB; Nesic I; Obradovic M; Stieltjes B; Weikert T; Bremerich J
articles.keywords: NLP; graph theory; radiology; imaging records; temporal referrals; date extraction; health care
articles.abstract: Background: A concise visualization framework of related reports would increase readability and improve patient management. Temporal referrals to prior comparative exams are an essential connection in written radiology reports. NLP permits extraction of structured information from unstructured texts. Objective: This study proposes and evaluates an NLP-based algorithm for extracting temporal referrals in written radiology reports, applies it to 10 years of reports, and introduces a graphical representation of imaging reports. Methods: A CNN extracted referral dates from reports (precision, recall, F1 measured on 149 reports). Results: The model reliably extracted temporal referrals. The graph-theoretic representation improved readability and clinical utility. Conclusion: Combining NLP with graph theory provides a novel framework for structuring and visualizing patient imaging histories.

articles.title: An Accurate and Efficient Approach to Knowledge Extraction from Scientific Publications Using Structured Ontology Models, GNNs, and LLMs
articles.authors: Ivanisenko TV; Demenkov PS; Ivanisenko VA
articles.keywords: text-mining; GNN; LLM; knowledge graph; biomedical; NLP; ontology; ANDSystem
articles.abstract: The rapid growth of biomedical literature challenges researchers to stay current. PubMed alone contains over 37 million abstracts. This paper presents a hybrid GNN-LLM method to extract knowledge from scientific publications using structured ontology models. Applied to insomnia, the approach identified 25 interactions between 32 human proteins absent from known knowledge bases, including regulatory interactions between MAOA and 5-HT2C and a circadian regulatory loop involving RORB and NR1D1. The method can accelerate therapeutic target discovery by focusing expert verification on automatically extracted, high-relevance information.

articles.title: Explainable Artificial Intelligence through Graph Theory by Generalized Social Network Analysis-Based Classifier
articles.authors: Not fully disclosed (Scientific Reports authors)
articles.keywords: explainable AI; graph theory; social network analysis; classifier; NLP; tabular data
articles.abstract: This paper proposes GSNAc, a supervised visual machine learning classifier based on graph theory and social network analysis (SNA). The model transforms tabular data into a network where samples are nodes and edges reflect pairwise similarities. The raw network graph is enriched and simplified to extract a visualizable Graph Classifier Model (GCM). Extended from the prior SNAc model for time-series data, GSNAc works with any tabular data including numerical and categorical features. NLP researchers can convert sentences into adjacency matrices based on word co-occurrence and apply graph similarity techniques for word similarity prediction. The model achieves competitive accuracy while providing inherent explainability through graph visualization.

articles.title: A Review of Graph Neural Networks: Concepts, Architectures, Techniques, Challenges, Datasets, Applications, and Future Directions
articles.authors: Not fully disclosed (Springer Nature Link)
articles.keywords: GNN; deep learning; NLP; text classification; sentiment analysis; social networks; fMRI
articles.abstract: Deep learning has seen significant growth and is now applied to graphs. This comprehensive review covers GNN concepts, architectures (GCN, GAT, GraphSAGE, GIN), training techniques, and open challenges. Applications span NLP (text sentiment analysis, text classification, question generation), social network analysis, biochemistry, and neuroscience (fMRI classification for major depressive disorder). Benchmark datasets and evaluation metrics are summarized. Future directions include scalable GNNs, dynamic graphs, heterogeneous graphs, and integration with LLMs for end-to-end NLP pipelines.

articles.title: Paths-over-Graph: Knowledge Graph Empowered Large Language Model Reasoning
articles.authors: Tan X; Wang X; Liu Q; Xu X; Yuan X; Zhang W
articles.keywords: LLM; knowledge graph; question answering; retrieval-augmented generation; graph reasoning; WWW 2025
articles.abstract: LLMs face challenges in answering complex questions requiring multi-hop reasoning due to knowledge incompleteness and hallucination. Paths-over-Graph (PoG) is a KG-empowered LLM reasoning framework that retrieves structured relation paths from KGs to guide multi-hop question answering. PoG introduces a novel path-searching algorithm that explores the KG efficiently by pruning irrelevant branches. Experiments on KGQA benchmarks (WebQSP, CWQ, MQA-3hop) show PoG achieves state-of-the-art results while producing interpretable reasoning chains as KG paths. Published at WWW '25, Sydney.

articles.title: Text Classification Using Graph Neural Networks: A Comparative Analysis
articles.authors: Wang K; Ding Y; Han SC
articles.keywords: GNN; text classification; NLP; graph construction; node features; transfer learning
articles.abstract: This paper presents a comparative analysis of graph-based text representation methods combined with GNN architectures for text classification. Twelve GNN architecture variants are evaluated using 3 message-passing layers and 4 pretrained word embedding techniques. Five prominent text classification datasets spanning diverse domains and including both short and longer documents are used. The study investigates efficacy versus efficiency trade-offs, an aspect neglected in prior work. Results show that graph methods generalize variably across datasets, and that ECAP-like cropping strategies improve handling of rare and long-tail classes.

articles.title: GraphER: A Structure-aware Text-to-Graph Model for Entity and Relation Extraction
articles.authors: Zaratiana U; Tomeh N; Holat P; Charnois T
articles.keywords: entity extraction; relation extraction; graph structure; NLP; information extraction; NAACL
articles.abstract: Information extraction (IE) is fundamental in NLP, with applications in knowledge graph construction. Prior models use separate or pipeline-based entity and relation prediction, limiting interaction. GraphER introduces a structure-aware text-to-graph model that enables unified prediction of entities and relations through tied, structure-informed decisions. By representing text as a graph and using graph-aware attention, the model captures structural dependencies overlooked by sequential models. Evaluated on joint entity and relation extraction benchmarks, GraphER achieves competitive state-of-the-art results with improved coherence between entity and relation predictions.

articles.title: The Power of Communities: A Text Classification Model with Automated Labeling Using Network Community Detection
articles.authors: Kim M; Sayama H
articles.keywords: community detection; text classification; Louvain method; TFIDF; graph theory; NLP; automated labeling
articles.abstract: Text classification performance depends heavily on the quality of human-labeled data. This study proposes a network community detection approach to automatically label and classify text into multiclass value spaces. Sentence networks are built where nodes are sentences and edge weights are pairwise cosine TFIDF similarities. The Louvain method detects communities serving as automated labels. SVM and Random Forest models are trained on both human-labeled and community-detected labels. Results showed models with community-detected labels outperformed human-labeled models by 2.68–3.75% accuracy, with potential applications in conversational intelligence systems.

articles.title: Towards Graph Prompt Learning: A Survey and Beyond
articles.authors: Long Q; Yan Y; Zhang P; Fang C; Cui W; Ning Z; et al.
articles.keywords: graph prompt learning; pre-train; GNN; NLP; CV; question answering; image recognition; survey
articles.abstract: Large-scale pre-train and prompt learning paradigms have demonstrated remarkable adaptability across diverse domains such as question answering, image recognition, and multimodal retrieval. This approach reduces downstream data requirements and computational costs. Despite success of prompt learning in NLP and CV, application in graph domains remains nascent. This survey summarizes methods for alleviating distribution disparities between graph pre-training and fine-tuning, covering prompt design methodologies, related techniques, application scenarios, datasets, and unresolved challenges. Over 100 relevant works are categorized, including text-attributed graphs, molecules, proteins, and recommendation systems.

articles.title: Scalable Community Extraction of Text Networks for Automated Grouping in Medical Databases
articles.authors: Sengupta S; et al. (NC State University)
articles.keywords: text networks; community detection; medical databases; spectral methods; NLP; modularity; graph theory
articles.abstract: Text networks represent large document databases where vertices are documents and edges represent pairwise similarity. Statistical network analysis techniques, particularly community detection, can group such databases into homogeneous clusters. This paper studies scalable community detection for text networks in medical databases. Methods include spectral approaches (Rohe, Jin, Sengupta-Chen) and modularity-based methods (Newman-Girvan). Theoretical guarantees for accuracy are discussed. The approach is applied to group biomedical literature, enabling automated discovery of thematic clusters without human annotation.

articles.title: GraphNarrator: Generating Textual Explanations for Graph Neural Networks
articles.authors: Not fully disclosed
articles.keywords: GNN; explainability; natural language explanation; self-explanation; LLM; Chain-of-Thought; NLP
articles.abstract: Traditional NLP explanation methods such as feature importance and saliency maps provide limited human-interpretable insights. This paper introduces GraphNarrator, a framework that generates natural language textual explanations for GNN predictions. The model combines self-explanation mechanisms with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting and zero-shot LLM reasoning to produce coherent, step-by-step explanations for graph classification decisions. Evaluated on multiple graph datasets, GraphNarrator outperforms explanation baselines in faithfulness and human preference scores, bridging the gap between GNN decisions and human understanding.
